questions.question: Differentiate between long-term and short-term scheduling.
keyPoints: Long-term scheduler (job scheduler) controls the degree of multiprogramming by selecting processes to load into memory from the disk.
Short-term scheduler (CPU scheduler) selects which process in the ready queue gets the CPU next.
Long-term scheduler runs much less frequently than the short-term scheduler.

questions.question: Consider the following set of processes with arrival time and burst time. Calculate the average waiting time using (a) FCFS scheduling and (b) Non-preemptive SJF scheduling. | Process | Arrival Time | Burst Time | | P1 | 0 | 8 | | P2 | 1 | 4 | | P3 | 2 | 9 | | P4 | 3 | 5 |
keyPoints: For FCFS: Draw Gantt chart in arrival order: P1 (0-8), P2 (8-12), P3 (12-21), P4 (21-26). Calculate waiting times: P1=0, P2=8-1=7, P3=12-2=10, P4=21-3=18. Average = (0+7+10+18)/4 = 8.75

For non-preemptive SJF: At time 0, only P1 is available. It runs until 8. Then, available processes are P2 (BT=4), P3 (BT=9), P4 (BT=5, arrives at 3). Shortest is P2. P2 runs 8-12. Then, available are P3 (BT=9) and P4 (BT=5). Shortest is P4. P4 runs 12-17. Finally, P3 runs 17-26.
Calculate SJF waiting times: P1=0, P2=8-1=7, P3=17-2=15, P4=12-3=9. Average = (0+7+15+9)/4 = 7.75
Conclusion: SJF provides a lower average waiting time than FCFS for this set, demonstrating its optimization.
